are_you_ok.title: 6 heat-related deaths in Cook County mark July as deadliest month in more than a decade
are_you_ok.summary: Heat-related deaths affecting broad Cook County population - genuine check-in about a discrete recent heat wave's toll.
are_you_ok.description: <p>Six people in Cook County have died from heat-related causes in July so far, marking the deadliest stretch of extreme hot-weather deaths since at least 2015.</p><p>Victor Ferguson, 48, is the latest person identified who died in the heat wave this month. Ferguson died July 2 from heat stroke with ethanol intoxication as a secondary cause, according to new records from the Cook County medical examiner’s office.</p><p>Four of the six people who died were younger than 60. In all, six July deaths have been logged in Cook County so far, the high temperature ranging from 88 to 95 degrees on the day they either died or were hospitalized, according to an analysis of National Weather Service data by WBEZ and the Chicago Sun-Times. During that first week of July, heading into the Fourth of July weekend, Cook County was under an extreme heat warning.</p><p>Besides Ferguson, the others whose deaths have been attributed to the heat are Stephanie Rodriguez, 45; David Gonzalez, 44; Barbara Whigham, 71; Mike Rodriguez, 57; and Daniel Machnikowski, 69, according to the medical examiner's office.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-3a0000" name="html-embed-module-3a0000"></a><div class="flourish-embed flourish-chart" data-src="visualisation/29744387"><script src="https://public.flourish.studio/resources/embed.js"></script><noscript><img src="https://public.flourish.studio/visualisation/29744387/thumbnail" width="100%" alt="chart visualization" /></noscript></div> </div> </div><p>Since Chicago’s 1995 heat wave that killed more than 700 people, the city has suffered through at least two other major deadly heat waves: <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2016/8/30/18354264/a-look-back-at-chicago-s-deadly-1995-heat-wave" target="_blank" ><u>in 1999, when 10 people died, and in 2012, when 22 died.</u></a></p><p>Marc Raifman, who works with the Extreme Weather Planners Coalition, said city officials should work with community members to examine the circumstances that led to the deaths this month.</p><p>“Why aren’t we looking at these cases, figuring out in some level of detail the same way they do in the medical field, what went wrong that led someone to experience a heat or cold injury that in some awful cases took their life?” Raifman asked. “And what can we do to prevent that next time and save the next person?”</p><p>Ferguson, the most recent heat-related fatality, lived in the East Garfield Park neighborhood on the West Side. His family could not be immediately reached. </p><p>Neighbors recalled Ferguson living with his family in a limestone two-flat home. Air conditioning units could be seen on the first and second floors. </p><p>Neighbors said Ferguson often was seen walking his two dogs.</p><p>Neighbors of Rodriguez said he shared a home with several relatives. Rodriguez, they added, lived in the attic, which didn't have great air circulation. An air conditioning unit could be seen mounted in an attic window Monday morning.</p><p>Relatives and friends of the others who died either declined to comment or couldn’t be reached, including a relative of Rodriguez who lived in the same home and declined to comment Monday.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a alt="The exterior of a small home where the Cook County Medical Examiner said Mike Rodriguez was living when he died from from heat-related complications in Mckinley Park. " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/263840c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F74%2F18%2Fe8b9101e4d3fabb0fc38c53a9c43%2F6heatdeaths-260721-03.JPG 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/22a267b/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F74%2F18%2Fe8b9101e4d3fabb0fc38c53a9c43%2F6heatdeaths-260721-03.JPG 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/263840c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F74%2F18%2Fe8b9101e4d3fabb0fc38c53a9c43%2F6heatdeaths-260721-03.JPG" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>The address where the Cook County Medical Examiner said Mike Rodriguez was living when he died from from heat-related complications in McKinley Park. A window air conditioner can be seen in the front window of the attic, where neighbors said Mike Rodriguez lived.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>One death happened in Evanston, and the other five people who died were residents of Chicago’s South and West sides. Communities in Chicago’s South and West sides are most vulnerable to extreme heat, said Daniel Horton, an associate professor at Northwestern University who helped develop a heat vulnerability index for the city.</p><p>Some of that vulnerability is tied to socioeconomic factors, like not being able to afford an elevated electricity bill to cool a home or not having an adequate air conditioning unit, Horton said. Neighborhoods with fewer trees also provide less shade during extreme heat, which is one reason community groups advocate for more shading near bus stops.</p><p>But a bigger issue to tackle is social isolation, Horton said, referencing one of the larger issues that came out of the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/city-hall/2015/7/11/18609460/chicago-1995-heat-wave-anniversary" target="_blank" ><u>devastating 1995 heat wave</u></a>. People were more likely to have died if they weren’t connected to neighbors who could check on them or give them a fan or other materials to help them stay cool.</p><p>“That social infrastructure piece of it is also really key to increasing a community’s resilience,” Horton said.</p><p>Community groups have also pushed not only for a 24-hour cooling center but also for one that stays open during weekends or holidays, Raifman said. The city has standards for activating an emergency response due to weather, but those standards can feel unattainable, he said.</p><p>The city sometimes extends closing hours to 8 p.m. for its community service center at 10 S. Kedzie Ave., according to the mayor’s office.</p><p>Mayor Brandon Johnson’s office also said officials were closely monitoring high temperatures, adding that they consider police districts, libraries, community colleges and park district facilities to be part of citywide cooling efforts. In a statement, the mayor’s office said it was “deeply saddened by any weather-related loss of life in our city,” and offered condolences.</p><p>“There are leaders and individuals at the city who are certainly working hard, in the moment, to address these things,” Raifman said. “But I think what they lack and what we lack is any kind of advance planning and understanding of what gets deployed where.”</p><p><i>Contributing: Cam Rodriguez, Mohammad Samra, Alden Loury</i></p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/weather/2026/07/10/fifth-heat-related-death-recorded-in-chicago-area" >Fifth heat-related death recorded in Chicago area</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2025/07/11/chicago-heat-wave-1995-deaths-brandon-johnson-northwestern-university-climate-change" >30 years after historic heat wave, Chicagoans are still at risk</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p>

conversation_starters.title: Chicago cabbies and ride-hailing drivers are both struggling amid changing industry: 'I'm stuck in this'
conversation_starters.summary: Substantive local feature on Chicago cabbies/rideshare struggles amid changing industry — widely relatable local-economy story, safe opener. You could open with: 'Did you read that piece on how Chicago cabbies are getting squeezed by the rideshare apps and now Waymo?'
conversation_starters.description: <p>The way Bob Singh sees it, the taxi industry has drastically changed, but he and other cabbies are still playing by the old rules, struggling to stay afloat as rideshare companies like Uber and Lyft dominate the market.</p><p>“Financially, it’s devastating,” Singh, 57, said.</p><p>But in the last few years, rideshare drivers have hit their own speed bumps. The app-based companies control their fares and the trips available to them, take an ever-changing percentage of their pay and sometimes cut off drivers’ accounts at a moment’s notice. City and state lawmakers recently took steps to support cabbies and rideshare drivers alike, but some worry it may be too little too late.</p><p>Since the pandemic, major commuting pattern shifts have hurt both taxis and rideshare services. And then there’s the emerging threat <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/transportation/2026/02/25/waymo-autonomous-vehicle-robotaxi-buckner-johnson" target="_blank" ><u>of autonomous vehicles</u></a> looming over all drivers.</p><p>Still, cabdrivers have had the roughest road to navigate as the industry evolves. The competition from rideshare apps has sharply reduced the demand for cabs and therefore the value of taxi medallions — the government-issued permit required for taxis. </p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7d0000" name="html-embed-module-7d0000"></a><div class="flourish-embed flourish-chart" data-src="visualisation/29662421"><script src="https://public.flourish.studio/resources/embed.js"></script><noscript><img src="https://public.flourish.studio/visualisation/29662421/thumbnail" width="100%" alt="chart visualization" /></noscript></div> </div> </div><p>In Chicago, the price of a taxi medallion peaked in 2012 at nearly $400,000, according to city data. Now, they go for around $5,000, which has left many Chicago cabbies in significant debt. </p><p>A taxi driver for 36 years, Singh said he feels “stuck” in the business. He was just a kid when his family moved from India to the U.S., and he started driving taxis at 21.</p><p>Singh bought his first medallion in 1991 for $20,000. Two decades later, when he and his brother bought another medallion, it was valued at $360,000. But as the market for those medallions has plummeted, they still owe around $490,000 on the loan, with a monthly payment of nearly $3,500.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Figure-credit"><p>Victor Hilitski/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>“[I'm] under a lot of stress," Singh said. "I had to put my kids [two sons] through college. One is still in college. It was not easy, but I mean, what can you do?”</p><p>On a typical day, Singh makes about $80 from six rides, though he’s scaled back his driving in recent years to focus on managing his other vehicles and spending more time with family. Based on conversations with the drivers he works with, he estimated that the average Chicago taxi driver makes around $20 per hour, if not less. An exact citywide average is not publicly available. </p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" data-embed> <iframe width="200" height="113" src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/c1F4j_DSZz0?feature=oembed" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share" referrerpolicy="strict-origin-when-cross-origin" allowfullscreen title="Can Chicago save its taxis? The fight to keep the meter running."></iframe> </div> </div></div><p></p><h3>‘An albatross around the neck of the taxicab industry’</h3><p>A July 1 fare hike of 20% was the first increase since 2016. When it was being considered, some speculated <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/city-hall/2026/04/16/chicago-cabbies-conflicted-about-fare-hike-intended-to-save-dying-taxi-industry" >it might reduce cab ridership</a>. While it's too early to assess its overall impact, after a couple of weeks Singh said his drivers "are happy."</p><p>"So far, I think it's a positive step," Singh said.</p><p>Cabdrivers say the pay bump was long overdue to help them keep up with rideshare competition and skyrocketing costs.</p><p>“Will this increase help? Yes, but it probably doesn’t even catch us up to what we’ve lost over the last 10, 12 years,” said John Moberg, a former driver and current president of Checker Taxi Affiliation and Yellow Cab.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-9f0000" name="image-9f0000"></a> <picture </figure> </div> </div><p>P.S. Sriraj, a transportation professor at the University of Illinois at Chicago, agreed with Moberg, saying the biggest issue in the taxicab industry is “not the operating cost. It’s the sunk cost.”</p><p>Medallions “are an albatross around the neck of the taxicab industry,” Sriraj said. “And those debts are still to be paid, and there is no wrangling out of that.”</p><p>Cabbies say that despite providing the same service, they’re placed on an unlevel playing field with rideshare drivers, who are not required to complete training or obtain a chauffeur license. </p><p>In most cases, rideshare drivers like Ebony Echevarria, 28, can apply online and start driving within a couple days.</p><p>“I was anxious the first time I took a ride because the whole time I felt like I was doing something wrong because it was so simple,” said Echevarria, a driver for Uber and Lyft for the last year and a half.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>In Chicago, rideshare companies operate in the city’s Transportation Network Provider program, <a class="Link" href="https://www.chicago.gov/content/dam/city/depts/mayor/Press%20Room/Press%20Releases/2014/May/05.28.14AppRideshare.pdf" target="_blank" ><u>created in 2014</u></a> to regulate app-based services.</p><p>As of May, about 85,000 rideshare drivers were registered in Chicago, according to city data. That compares with about 2,600 active taxi licenses in the city.</p><p>Nearly a decade ago, in 2017, there were almost 25 million total taxi rides in the city. But as <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/news/2014/06/05/rideshare-vs-taxicabs-the-inside-story" target="_blank" >rideshare companies gained a foothold</a> in Chicago, the market was upended.</p><p>In November and December 2018, the first two months of available rideshare data, the companies racked up 17.5 million rides, almost as many rides as cabs had that entire year. The following year, rideshare riders made nearly 112 million trips, the most ever. Meanwhile, taxi ridership dropped 21%.</p><p>Fast forward to this year. On an average day in May, Chicago riders took about 22,000 cab rides — but about 250,000 rideshare trips. That's despite cab rides being typically cheaper per ride compared to a rideshare service, based on an analysis of city data before the new taxi fare hike went into effect this month.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-040000" name="html-embed-module-040000"></a><div class="flourish-embed flourish-chart" data-src="visualisation/29728790"><script src="https://public.flourish.studio/resources/embed.js"></script><noscript><img src="https://public.flourish.studio/visualisation/29728790/thumbnail" width="100%" alt="chart visualization" /></noscript></div> </div> </div><p></p><h3><b>A day in the life of a cabbie</b></h3><p>Singh’s day usually starts at 3:30 a.m. when he awakes at his home in Palos Park.</p><p>He drives his yellow Dodge Caravan to Chicago for the first of three regular customers he drives to work each weekday. Pershing Road in McKinley Park. The facility opens at 8 a.m., and by that time, a dozen or so vehicles are lined up and waiting as cars get a thorough inspection one at a time.</p><p>Rideshare drivers, on the other hand, are required to have only one vehicle inspection a year. They’re typically done through a private mechanic and are not as onerous as taxicab inspections.</p><p>Singh’s inspection lasts about 30 minutes, but his two hours at the inspection office is time not spent picking up passengers. class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Passenger Fan Sun rides in Bob Singh’s taxi through Chicago in June.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Victor Hilitski/For the Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3>‘They are scraping by right now’</h3><p>Despite its pole position in the market, rideshare driving comes with its own set of challenges.</p><p>While the barriers to entry are lower than the taxi industry, many rideshare drivers find themselves earning modest incomes while remaining heavily dependent on platforms that dictate the terms of their work.</p><p>That led to a push by rideshare drivers to <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/springfield/2026/06/04/illinois-general-assembly-bill-rideshare-uber-lyft-drivers-unionize" target="_blank" ><u>pass a state law in May</u></a> allowing them to form unions. The bill awaits Gov. JB Pritzker’s signature.</p><p>“Because affordability is at the top of everyone’s mind with gas prices rising, drivers really can’t wait. They are scraping by right now,” said Bailey Koch, spokesperson for the Illinois Drivers Alliance. “This is their full-time job, it’s how they pay their bills, and they just cannot keep up. And there’s absolutely no way they can make a change in their workplace without forming a union.”</p><p>In a statement, a spokesperson for Uber said the company supports the state law "because it preserves the flexibility that drivers consistently tell us they value most while creating a statewide framework for benefits, representation and workplace protections."</p><p>A spokesperson for Lyft said the company looks forward to "working collaboratively" on the bill's implementations and "addressing some of the rider impact concerns we have during rule making later this year."</p><div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Ebony Echevarria, rideshare driver for Uber and Lyft.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Echevarria, who lives in Rogers Park, drives long enough each day to at least earn the money needed to fill her 2023 Honda CR-V with gas, which has increased lately.</p><p>Before taking on a bigger role this year with the Illinois Drivers Alliance, Echevarria would drive about 12 hours a day — from 7 p.m. to 7 a.m. while someone was available to watch her young daughter.</p><p>On average, she makes about $20 per hour, driving roughly 20 hours per week. </p><p>“It’s impressive if you’re able to make over $200 in a day, and that’s before the expenses of taking care of your vehicle and before gas,” Echevarria said.</p><p>The AI-driven algorithms that rideshare apps use to determine prices act like a reverse lottery system that results in customers paying less but also drivers earning less money. A ride request will go to whichever driver in the area accepts the lowest fare.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-1f0000" name="image-1f0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/40ad4d4/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1024x575+0+54/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ffb%2F7a%2Fe22f46454e47bf0d17dc2d85c6a9%2Ftaxirideshare-260707-14.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/93b4c7c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1024x575+0+54/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ffb%2F7a%2Fe22f46454e47bf0d17dc2d85c6a9%2Ftaxirideshare-260707-14.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/8c54d11/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1024x575+0+54/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ffb%2F7a%2Fe22f46454e47bf0d17dc2d85c6a9%2Ftaxirideshare-260707-14.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="A sticker for the Uber and Illinois Driver Alliance on the windshield of Ebony Echevarria, who drives for Uber and Lyft. Photographed on Friday, July 3, 2026." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/8c54d11/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1024x575+0+54/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ffb%2F7a%2Fe22f46454e47bf0d17dc2d85c6a9%2Ftaxirideshare-260707-14.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a452f76/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1024x575+0+54/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ffb%2F7a%2Fe22f46454e47bf0d17dc2d85c6a9%2Ftaxirideshare-260707-14.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/8c54d11/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1024x575+0+54/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ffb%2F7a%2Fe22f46454e47bf0d17dc2d85c6a9%2Ftaxirideshare-260707-14.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>A sticker for the Uber and Illinois Driver Alliance on the windshield of Ebony Echevarria, who drives for Uber and Lyft</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The driver doesn’t take all of that fare. Rideshare companies take a cut, but the exact percentage can vary and is not disclosed. A spokesperson for Uber said, "A significant and growing portion of the fare goes toward covering insurance costs."</p><p>Lyft takes about 14% of what passengers pay for a ride, and the company caps that share at 30%, according to a company spokesperson. After Lyft takes its share, then an undisclosed amount goes toward insurance, taxes and government-mandated fees, the driver takes home the rest.</p><p>“Unfortunately, there is not a real breakdown of [pay],” Koch said. “That is, I think, what’s really frustrating because there’s no transparent reason as to why a ride is a certain amount of money. And, say there’s a surcharge because of traffic or whatever else, drivers aren’t necessarily getting a bigger cut of that pie.”</p><p>Another big concern for rideshare drivers is sudden deactivations due to a rider complaint or poor rating.</p><p>“In the blink of an eye, a driver could be kicked off the app and not have an income,” said Koch, with the Illinois Drivers Alliance. “And there’s no way for them to give their side of the story, to have a process to get their job back, and usually when they’re reaching out, they’re not even talking to humans to try to repeal it, so they’re just stuck.”</p><h3><b>‘What am I going to do?’</b></h3><p>For the taxi industry to survive and coexist with rideshare services, legal reforms for cabbies and more regulations for rideshares are needed, said Sriraj, the UIC professor.</p><p>Chicago could implement a medallion debt relief and restructuring program like New York City did several years ago, he suggested. Outstanding medallion mortgages were reduced to $170,000, and monthly loan payments were capped at around $1,200.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" He remains in the business “until hopefully it will get better and then one day, hopefully, I can retire.”</p><p>“I’m stuck in this because I have no choice. I mean, what am I going to do? I’m 57 years old, where else am I gonna go?” Singh said. “Just roll with the punches and see how it goes."</p><p>Still, the jovial taxi driver, whose favorite part of the job is talking with customers, has learned to keep things in perspective.</p><p>After driving for so many years, “You realize money’s not everything,” Singh said. “Health is really important. June.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Victor Hilitski/For the Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>For Echevarria and other rideshare drivers in Illinois, the future is just as uncertain but a little less gloomy.</p><p>Forming a union could help rebalance their relationship with rideshare companies. And it might provide some protections from the growing autonomous vehicle industry led by companies like Waymo.</p><p>“In the same way that the Uber and Lyft drivers have taken away from cabdrivers, those [autonomous vehicles] are trying to take away from us again,” Echevarria said. “So imagine in one generation being a cabdriver that then has had your job taken away [by] Uber, and then you become an Uber driver, and then you have your job taken away [by] Waymo.” </p><p><i>Contributing: Cam Rodriguez</i></p>

conversation_starters.title: St. Vincent explores ‘new dimension’ in orchestra collabs
conversation_starters.summary: St. Vincent playing with the CSO at Ravinia this weekend — marquee-ish local arts event and safe opener. Borderline prominence. You could open with: 'Did you see St. Vincent is playing with the Chicago Symphony at Ravinia this weekend?'
conversation_starters.description: <p>Musician Annie Clark, the multi-instrumentalist who performs under the name St. Vincent, is no stranger to reinvention.  </p><p>Much like her creative heroes — David Bowie, Prince, <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/theater-stages/2026/02/05/david-byrne-chicago-theater-of-the-mind-goodman-talking-heads" target="_blank" ><u>David Byrne</u></a> — Clark’s sound and stage presence have evolved throughout the nearly 20 years since her debut album, “Marry Me,” was released in 2007. Now Clark continues to defy genres by appearing with a full orchestra, including a performance with the <a class="Link" href="https://www.ravinia.org/events/detail/st-vincent-cso-260725" target="_blank" ><u>Chicago Symphony Orchestra this weekend at Ravinia</u></a>.</p><div class="Enhancement FormEnhancement" data-align-center> <div id="Form-newsletter-wrapper" York (which she calls “my heart’s home”), Los Angeles and her native Texas.</p><p>The “St. Vincent Live With Orchestra” project debuted last fall at London’s Royal Albert Hall as part of the longstanding BBC Proms series and was released as a live album in March. The reimagined versions of songs from across Clark’s catalog — including “Paris is Burning” and “Smoking Section” — were brought to life in collaboration with the composer and arranger Jules Buckley, whom Clark was introduced to through her longtime friend and keyboardist Rachel Eckroth.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-8a0000" name="module-8a0000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3><a class="Link" href="https://www.ravinia.org/events/detail/st-vincent-cso-260725" target="_blank" ><b><u>St. Vincent with the Chicago Symphony Orchestra</u></b></a></h3>6 p.m., Saturday, July 25<br>Ravinia Hunter Pavilion<br>Tickets from $55</div> </div><p>This summer, Clark, Buckley and a small band of players — including Eckroth — have taken the show on the road for a tour with local orchestras that began in Pittsburgh in June, wound through Boston and New York, and will come to the Chicago suburbs before concluding in August at the Hollywood Bowl.</p><p>The project gave Clark a chance to dig through her archive for songs ripe for orchestral rearrangement and spotlight some of her lesser-known early work, which she says was inspired by composers like Igor Stravinsky.</p><p>“It all kind of started [by] going ‘all right, which songs in the catalog are going to be made better, cooler with a new dimension with the symphony?’” Clark said. “I definitely wanted to honor some of the songs from the earlier records that I haven't touched in a really long time and give them their flowers.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-750000" name="image-750000"></a> <picture Here, she performs at Riot Fest in 2024. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Ashlee Rezin/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>When we spoke in mid-June, Clark was in New York between tour dates. During the interview for this story, Clark was walking around the city when a man stopped her and asked for money. After she sweetly told him she didn’t have any cash on her, she ducked into a bodega, offering to buy him the pizza and Hawaiian punch he selected. “I got you, baby,” she said.</p><p>Back outside, she said into the phone, “There's this kid, you know, he's strung out. He needed something.”</p><p>And then, seamlessly, she dove back into talking about her music. “In this weird way, this concert became kind of like — I'm not nearly dead, thank God — but it became sort of a midlife career retrospective,” she said.</p><p>While the orchestral project is a totally new endeavor for the six-time Grammy-Award-winning Clark, it puts her in the company of many pop peers, who have also recently waded into the world of classical collaborations.</p><p>There's Rosalía, whose latest album, “Lux,” was recorded with the London Symphony Orchestra. And in Chicago this summer, <a class="Link" href="https://chicagophilharmonic.org/events/lucy-dacus/?l=L1&ri=0" target="_blank" ><u>Lucy Dacus</u></a> and <a class="Link" href="https://www.grantparkmusicfestival.com/events/an-evening-with-ben-folds/" target="_blank" ><u>Ben Folds</u></a> are among the rock singers appearing with the Chicago Philharmonic and Grant Park Orchestra, respectively, while <a class="Link" href="https://www.ravinia.org/events/detail/hunter-pavilion-cso-90-opening-260711" target="_blank" ><u>Lizzo</u></a> and her flute helped kick off CSO’s season at Ravinia, and hometown artist <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/arts-culture/2026/02/24/andrew-bird-debut-chicago-symphony-orchestra" target="_blank" ><u>Andrew Bird</u></a> will appear with the orchestra this fall.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-d50000" class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Lizzo is another artist collaborating with a Chicago orchestra this summer. Here, she Lizzo performs Giulio Briccialdi’s “Carnival of Venice” with the Chicago Symphony Orchestra on the opening night of Ravinia Festival.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Ashlee Rezin/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Another prominent local example of rock-orchestra collaborations was <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/music/2025/11/18/billy-corgan-smashing-pumpkins-night-of-mellon-collie-infinite-sadness-lyric-opera" target="_blank" ><u>Billy Corgan</u></a>’s reimagination of the Smashing Pumpkins’ hit album “Mellon Collie and the Infinite Sadness.” Last fall, he debuted the project, backed by the Lyric Opera’s full orchestra and chorus, in a sold-out run of seven shows.</p><p>In the Corgan project, the songs were reimagined solely as symphonic works, without guitar or drum parts. In the St. Vincent universe, Clark does retain the role of world-class guitar shredder (Rolling Stone named <a class="Link" href="https://www.rollingstone.com/music/music-lists/best-guitarists-1234814010/st-vincent-19-1234814295/" target="_blank" ><u>Clark the 26th greatest guitarist of all time</u></a>). She is joined onstage by her touring guitarist, bassist, drummer and keyboardist, in addition to the members of the orchestra.</p><p>While the trend of popular-star-goes-classical seems to be everywhere, it’s hardly new. In 1999, Metallica brought their heavy metal sound to a collaboration with the San Francisco Symphony on a series of live shows that resulted in the five-time platinum album “S&M.”</p><p>Still, the need for these types of crossover programs seems more urgent, as arts administrators in the classical universe look to attract broader audiences in the wake of the COVID-19 pandemic.</p><p>CSO bassist Dan Carson said he understands the need to fill out a season with shows that have broad, mainstream appeal. When we spoke, Carson and his colleagues were fresh off their final shows at Symphony Center for the season: A three-night run of “Star Wars: The Empire Strikes Back,” in which the orchestra played John Williams’ score live.</p><p>He knows that these types of events, whether Downtown or at Ravinia, are a way to sell tickets. And while he said, “We obviously love Mozart and Mahler,” he also said there’s something about the buzz of a sold-out audience — dressed up as Princess Leia or Stormtroopers — that is infectious. “I want people to go nuts,” Carson said. “I really like the energy.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-b40000" name="image-b40000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f90ede7/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+130/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fda%2Fe0%2Fdea2b0db4566945357921d60efd4%2Flollasat-080418-8.png 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/cae5241/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+130/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fda%2Fe0%2Fdea2b0db4566945357921d60efd4%2Flollasat-080418-8.png 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/9ed7e83/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+130/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fda%2Fe0%2Fdea2b0db4566945357921d60efd4%2Flollasat-080418-8.png" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="St. Vincent performs at Lollapalooza in 2018. " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/9ed7e83/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+130/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fda%2Fe0%2Fdea2b0db4566945357921d60efd4%2Flollasat-080418-8.png 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/952312c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+130/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fda%2Fe0%2Fdea2b0db4566945357921d60efd4%2Flollasat-080418-8.png 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/9ed7e83/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+130/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fda%2Fe0%2Fdea2b0db4566945357921d60efd4%2Flollasat-080418-8.png" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Clark said that when she arrives in a new city, she likes “the process of slowly winning orchestras over,” but there’s typically only one rehearsal to do it.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Tyler Pasciak LaRiviere/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>And when artists of other genres — whether jazz singer <a class="Link" href="https://cso.org/about/performers/visiting-artists/vocals/samara-joy/" target="_blank" ><u>Samara Joy</u></a> or bluegrass-rooted <a class="Link" href="https://cso.org/performances/25-26/scp-featured-concerts/an-evening-with-chris-thile/" target="_blank" ><u>Chris Thile</u></a> — come to town, Carson said there’s generally a mutual respect. Though he said sometimes the music retrofitted for orchestral players isn’t so challenging.</p><p>“Something that's common is that they'll have an electric bass player, so having the orchestral bass in addition to that is almost a little bit redundant,” Carson said, “so we'll just kind of add some maybe pizzicato, some plucking to give it some texture or something.”</p><p>Clark said that when she arrives in a new city, she likes “the process of slowly winning orchestras over,” but there’s typically only one rehearsal to do it.</p><p>“I like seeing them transition from ‘Oh man, when's this going to be over?’ to ‘We had a good time with nice, talented people, and we enjoyed that.’”</p><p>For Clark, the way to do that is by being respectful; plus, she said, “The charts are really good.”</p><p>“It's so beautiful. I mean, 60 incredible musicians playing music conducted by a great conductor,” Clark said. “When do 60 people come together to do anything? I just get to bathe in beauty.”</p><p><i>Courtney Kueppers is an arts and culture reporter at WBEZ. </i></p>

you_should_know.title: En audiencia, la Policía de Chicago dice que ha implementado la orden del alcalde para investigar a ICE
you_should_know.summary: Institutional/political story on CPD's ICE-oversight order implementation; local ICE-enforcement climate context, keep as you-should-know (same event as item 18).
you_should_know.description: <p>Funcionarios de la Ciudad de Chicago fueron convocados a una tensa audiencia del comité del Concejo Municipal el viernes para brindar respuestas sobre la implementación tardía de la orden del alcalde Brandon Johnson que instruye al Departamento de Policía de Chicago (CPD) a investigar posibles delitos cometidos por agentes federales de inmigración.</p><p>Los miembros del Comité de Derechos de Inmigrantes y Refugiados pasaron aproximadamente dos horas interrogando a los asistentes del alcalde y a los líderes policiales sobre la orden ejecutiva de Johnson, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2026/07/15/orden-alcalde-chicago-contra-agentes-enfrenta-fuerte-escrutinio-concejales" target="_blank" >“ICE on Notice” (“ICE sobre aviso”)</a>, para saber qué ha logrado el decreto y qué pasos adicionales son necesarios para hacerla cumplir.</p><p>Los funcionarios de la policía dijeron que el departamento había concluido los procedimientos internos para implementar la orden, casi cinco meses después del <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2026/02/02/alcalde-de-chicago-ordena-al-departamento-de-policia-investigar-a-los-agentes-federales-de-inmigracion" target="_blank" >plazo de 30 días</a> establecido por el alcalde. Sin embargo, no quisieron explicar por qué tomó tanto tiempo.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-780000" name="module-780000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>La Voz Chicago WhatsApp</h3>Encuentra más noticias en nuestro canal de WhatsApp. <a class="Link" href="https://www.whatsapp.com/channel/0029Vb0XT1XCHDylm3rCs40P" target="_blank" ><b>Síguenos.</b></a></div> </div><p>“Valoramos a CPD y queremos creer que los oficiales están aquí para preservar y proteger nuestras comunidades. Pero ya es hora de que haya responsabilidad ante el público”, dijo el concejal Andre Vasquez (40.°), presidente del comité.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Horas antes, Johnson emitió un comunicado de prensa instando a las personas a denunciar alegaciones de irregularidades derivadas del <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2025/09/08/trump-chicago-crime-pritzker-national-guard-ice-truth-social?utm_medium=email&utm_campaign=090925%20Morning%20Edition&utm_content=090925%20Morning%20Edition+CID_8198ca4c8936998efce45f5340df929e&utm_source=cst_campaign_monitor&utm_term=Trump%20launches%20long-promised%20Chicago%20deportation%20campaign&tpcc=cst_cm" target="_blank" >Operativo Midway Blitz</a>, la campaña de deportación agresiva de la administración de Trump el otoño pasado.</p><p>Pero el llamado hizo poco para calmar las frustraciones de los concejales y de los miembros de la comunidad.</p><p>Los miembros del comité llegaron preparados con una lista de preguntas: ¿Cómo hará cumplir el departamento de policía las nuevas políticas? ¿Ha sido notificado todo el departamento de los cambios? ¿Y quién puede presentar un reporte de policía si alguien es detenido por un agente federal?.</p><p>Los funcionarios de la policía dijeron que el departamento carecía de un sistema para rastrear las quejas contra agentes federales. Eso significa que tuvieron que establecer nuevos protocolos para investigar esas quejas, recolectar evidencia e identificar a los agentes federales involucrados.</p><p>Se les ha indicado a los oficiales que obtengan declaraciones de testigos y presenten informes, pero el departamento no abrirá casos criminales a menos que una víctima o un familiar de la víctima se presente.</p><p>“Debemos trabajar de acuerdo con los deseos de la víctima y con lo que esta quiere que hagamos, ya sea continuar con el informe de caso realizado o no”, dijo Patrick Kinney, comandante de la Oficina de Detectives.</p><p>Si una víctima no puede presentar un informe, un familiar puede hacerlo en su lugar, dijeron los funcionarios. Eso incluye casos en los que alguien es detenido por agentes federales y los miembros de su familia no pueden localizarlo. La policía puede iniciar una investigación sobre la conducta del agente y las circunstancias que rodean la detención.</p><p>En el comunicado de prensa del viernes, la oficina de Johnson indicó que el departamento de policía está revisando un informe emitido en abril por la <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2026/05/04/comision-que-investiga-el-operativo-midway-blitz-sienta-las-bases-para-una-verdadera-rendicion-de-cuentas" target="_blank" >Comisión de Responsabilidad de Illinois</a>, convocada por el gobernador JB Pritzker para investigar abusos cometidos por agentes del Operativo Midway Blitz. Se instó a las personas nombradas en el informe —y a otros que “fueron testigos o experimentaron conductas ilegales”— a ponerse en contacto con el departamento de policía.</p><p>Durante la ofensiva de cumplimiento del año pasado, residentes de la ciudad —e incluso policías— fueron <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2025/11/13/tear-gas-federal-agents-immigration-enforcement-chicago-police-department-dan-oconnor" target="_blank" >expuestos a gases lacrimógenos</a>. Los agentes federales <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2025/11/14/ice-bovino-border-patrol-homeland-security-chicago-police-department" target="_blank" >provocaron accidentes de tráfico</a> mientras realizaban arrestos. Una <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2026/02/04/marimar-martinez-baleada-por-agentes-de-migracion-se-pronuncia-contra-asesinatos" target="_blank" >mujer fue baleada cinco veces</a> en el lado suroeste de la ciudad y un padre inmigrante murió <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2025/11/18/silverio-villegas-gonzalez-asesinado-por-agente-de-ice-en-area-de-chicago-tenia-cocaina-en-el-sistema-autopsia" target="_blank" >tras ser herido de muerte</a> en el suburbio de Franklin Park.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-52d55e" class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Las autoridades de la Policía de Chicago responden preguntas el viernes sobre la implementación de la orden ejecutiva “ICE sobre aviso” del alcalde Brandon Johnson durante una audiencia del comité del Concejo Municipal.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Los funcionarios de la policía dijeron que están buscando testigos y pruebas. Indicaron que utilizarán imágenes de las cámaras de la policía y de los lectores de placas para investigar los informes de delitos.</p><p>Y mientras afirmaron que cierta información podría ser divulgada públicamente, la evidencia y otros materiales de investigación seguirán siendo confidenciales para preservar la integridad de cualquier posible enjuiciamiento penal.</p><p>“Nuestra mayor preocupación es asegurarnos de que cualquier evidencia que se nos presente pueda presentarse en la corte”, dijo Kinney. “La cadena de custodia es extremadamente importante para nosotros”.</p><p>La audiencia se llevó a cabo en medio de lo que los defensores locales han descrito como <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2026/07/16/defensores-de-inmigrantes-en-chicago-exigen-abolir-ice-tras-tiroteos-mortales-en-houston-y-maine" target="_blank" >un aumento en la aplicación de la ley de inmigración</a> y tras dos tiroteos fatales que involucraron a agentes del Servicio de Inmigración y Control de Aduanas (ICE) en Texas y Maine.</p><p>Ningún agente de inmigración ha sido acusado de cometer delitos en el ejercicio de sus funciones en el Condado de Cook, a pesar de los fuertes llamados a la rendición de cuentas. La fiscal del estado del Condado de Cook, Eileen O’Neill Burke, ha sostenido que le corresponde a la policía investigar y remitir los casos a su oficina para su acusación.</p><p>Esa postura fue respaldada por la jueza del circuito del Condado de Cook, Erica Reddick, quien rechazó una solicitud para <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2026/05/26/jueza-del-condado-de-cook-rechaza-nombrar-fiscal-especial-para-investigar-agentes-de-operativo-midway-blitz" target="_blank" >designar a un fiscal especial</a> para investigar delitos alegados por agentes federales de inmigración.</p><p><i>Traducido con una herramienta de inteligencia artificial (AI) y editado por </i><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz" target="_blank" ><i>La Voz Chicago</i></a></p>

you_should_know.title: Illinois Republican Party chair Bob Grogan aims to shake off 'Stockholm syndrome' of struggling state GOP
you_should_know.summary: Profile of new IL GOP chair — partisan/horse-race-ish, but state political context; on the context-vs-noise boundary, keep as you should know. Not a safe opener.
you_should_know.description: <p>It doesn’t take a professional auditor to conclude the Illinois Republican Party is stuck in a rut, but the state GOP is hoping one can get them back on the path to relevance.</p><p>Nearly 12 years removed from their last statewide election victory and long relegated to super-minority status in Springfield, Republicans have tapped former DuPage County Auditor Bob Grogan to chair the state party, aiming to revitalize the suburban base that has largely abandoned the GOP under President Donald Trump.</p><p>Grogan spent 12 years as an elected watchdog keeping tabs on various arms of the county government, and he has no misconceptions about his new volunteer post recruiting candidates, fundraising and, most importantly, attempting to unite a fractured party.</p><p>“You get blamed for everything and credit for nothing,” Grogan told the Sun-Times. “I'm not in it for the glory. I like to say professionally, what I do is I fix broken things. And that's my audit background, where you find problems, and you don't play ‘gotcha.’ You just say, ‘Here’s a list of solutions. Let's try to go out and do it.’”</p><p>He’ll need a long list in the wake of his predecessor, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/politics/2024/07/12/kathy-salvi-illinois-republican-party-chair" target="_blank" ><u>Kathy Salvi</u></a>, who wasn’t able to make many statewide inroads before Grogan unseated her in a May vote by the Republican State Central Committee.</p><p>“She was more aspirational, and I'm more operational,” said Grogan, who doesn’t embrace Trump quite as enthusiastically as Salvi did. Grogan compared the president to an “elephant in the china shop.”</p><p>“We share a lot of the same goals. We definitely don't share a style,” the Downers Grove resident said, referring to Trump. “I come from a totally different background. I'm a boring CPA. He's a reality TV show, New York real estate developer … But he's got the hardest job in the world, and I probably have the second hardest job in the world, being chairman of the Illinois Republican Party.”</p><p>Trump has certainly complicated matters for the state party in the suburbs, which provided the lifeblood of the Illinois GOP for generations. Former President Joe Biden won DuPage County by 18 percentage points in 2024.</p><p>Grogan contends the “evolution in the suburbs” started under former President Barack Obama. </p><p>But he acknowledges the headwinds the MAGA movement has brought to suburbs that used to be reliably red.</p><p>“[Trump] brought in blue-collar voters that weren't ours before, and that's a good thing and it's a challenging thing,” Grogan said. “Blue-collar voters need to be enticed a little bit more. They need to be excited a little bit more." </p><p>Grogan doesn’t push Trump’s false conspiracy theory of widespread voter fraud costing him the 2020 presidential election, and Grogan says his party needs to embrace mail voting the president has sowed doubt about.</p><p>But the new chair is quick to note “no system is zero-fraud and zero-error” — including the one in DuPage County that saw him lose his bid for a third term as auditor.</p><p>“In 2020, I lost by 76 votes out of a half a million, and I will tell you that the error rate was probably north of that, because we threw out over 800 ballots,” Grogan said. “People should know that every vote does count, and we want to count every vote accurately.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/79de3c8/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8192x4598+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fcb%2Fff%2F010ada2a4eea933946ed2a7e0479%2Fgrogan-2609020077.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/02f8adc/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8192x4598+0+0/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fcb%2Fff%2F010ada2a4eea933946ed2a7e0479%2Fgrogan-2609020077.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/79de3c8/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8192x4598+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fcb%2Fff%2F010ada2a4eea933946ed2a7e0479%2Fgrogan-2609020077.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Illinois Republican Party chair Bob Grogan, pictured at Chicago Public Media offices. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Manuel Martinez/WBEZ</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>As for abortion rights that remain popular and important to many suburban voters, Grogan, who calls himself pro-life, decried Illinois as “an abortion destination state, and I don't think that's what the average suburban, pro-choice person really wants.</p><p>“They don't want late-term abortions. They don't want taxpayer-funded abortions for folks that aren't even citizens,” Grogan said. “Abortion tourism is not something that they would really endorse.”</p><p>And he’s undaunted by the odds playing on heavily gerrymandered, Democratic-drawn maps.</p><p>“Voter patterns change, and they evolve. I have optimism … that eventually governance matters, and I think that the governance of the state of Illinois, and city of Chicago, leaves a lot to be desired,” Grogan said. “That old definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over again, expecting a different result.”</p><p>But Republicans are running Darren Bailey again this November against Gov. JB Pritzker, four years after the southern Illinois farmer got trounced by the billionaire governor who’s now heavily favored to win a third term.</p><p>Grogan backed Ted Dabrowski <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/elections/2026/02/23/illinois-republican-primary-governor-candidates-preview-bailey-dabrowski-heidner-mendrick" target="_blank" ><u>in the GOP primary</u></a> and now says Bailey “has definitely come back at it with a different approach … We have structural problems. But if you only believed polls, Hillary Clinton would be president.”</p><p>More importantly, Grogan said, “There is definitely a lot more JB fatigue than there was six years ago.</p><p>“You bring up the governor's name, and there's a grimace. Where they were giving the benefit of the doubt a couple cycles ago, they're not so happy,” he said, pointing to COVID-19 business restrictions under Pritzker early in the pandemic and the governor’s potential White House ambitions in 2028.</p><p>“A lot of people are getting the impression he's looking over the person's shoulder at New Hampshire, and the recipe for New Hampshire and Iowa is not the recipe for Illinois,” said Grogan, who’s tasked with working up a fundraising rebound, too.</p><p>The Democratic Party of Illinois’ $1.2 million cash on hand at the end of June roughly quadrupled the state GOP’s total. Meanwhile, Bailey entered the summer with about $128,000 in his campaign fund compared to more than $3 million for Pritzker, who can cut another massive check anytime he pleases.</p><p>“We've had an over-reliance on one or two donors on the Republican side, which means that we've been lazy on developing infrastructure for small-dollar donors and such that really should be our bedrock,” Grogan said, referencing the likes of conservative megadonor Richard Uihlein.</p><p>A lot of it will take basic leg work and candidate recruitment. “Even if they aren't going to win someplace, and obviously some ... districts are really hard, I think that a well-crafted dialogue with voters has up-ticket and down-ticket effects,” Grogan said. “The first step is they have to meet us, and then they’ve got to hear us, and then they'll like us. And you can't do that just by listening to the screamers on Facebook.”</p><p>It’s also about changing the mindset of Republicans who “kind of suffer from almost a Stockholm syndrome, that they've been so long outside of power that they no longer identify [with] what it's like … to get power,” Grogan said.</p><p>“So we argue about how many angels dance on the head of a pin, as opposed to how to most advantageously take back a House seat,” he said. “Fratricide is not a great campaign strategy."</p><p>“We are in the super minority, and we've been there for a long time. We haven't held statewide office for quite a few years now. But the same was said 40 years ago — like the governor's mansion was ours exclusively,” Grogan said.</p><p>“There's no magic flipping of a switch … We actually have to not fight about things that aren't really as determinative to the average person trying to make their mortgage payment or their rent payment,” Grogan said. “We have prescriptions for many of the ills. At some point, I think the voters are going to stop digging deeper and look out of the hole that they've dug themselves into.”</p>
